ground_truth.title: Netiquette & Best Practices: Make the Most of Online Learning
ground_truth.author: Anthe Mitrakos
ground_truth.main_content:
Welcome to the Fall semester from Technology Solutions! Remotely-held courses sure have their pros and challenges. The quality of your interactions with instructors and classmates have a major impact on your experience as an online student. Technology Solutions is here to help you navigate the online environment with some Netiquette tips!

FOLLOW NETIQUETTE:
The quality of your interactions with instructors and classmates have a major impact on your experience as an online student.

1. Review & follow instructor communication guidelines. Be sure to review your course syllabus and any emails, instruction or announcements from your professor to familiarize yourself with expectations and avoid confusion.
2. Select a quiet space to study & meet from. To the best of your abilities, make sure that the space you select for your online classes is quiet and as free of distractions and background noise as possible.
3. Actively participate & introduce yourself. Keep engaged through speaking or chat. Note your name the first few times you speak to let moderators and other session attendees know who is participating.
4. Mute yourself when not speaking. This reduces background noise such as sirens, typing, barking, eating, or a private conversation, that can distract the session.
5. Use a civil tone & non-offensive language. NEVER use caps. While you are encouraged to think critically and engage in rigorous debate, please avoid using personal attacks, crude language and CAPS.
6. Be cautious when using humor or sarcasm. With online communication, be more polite than you might be in person. It's quite easy for misunderstandings to develop online because you aren't able to use tone of voice (audio) or facial expression (video).
7. Write clearly & succinctly. Aim to make your messages easy to read and understand.
8. Stay focused on your online courses by avoiding extra distractions. Separate your online course-related computer time use with that of your personal use.
9. Attribute any sources you use. Doing so should make your comments more effective while also encouraging a more rigorous and structured debate.
10. Be professional & respect others. Be mindful of your peers and instructors. Don't talk over others. Raise your hand if there is no opening to speak.

AVOID TECHNICAL PROBLEMS:
Be ready for online courses! Review these troubleshooting tips to ensure your computer is ready for online courses.

1. Join the session early. Join the session 5-10 minutes early to ensure you can access the session and resolve any technical issues.
2. Know your online learning tools. Before your class, get acquainted with the online collaboration tools your professors will be using (Blackboard Collaborate, Zoom, Microsoft Teams, etc.)
3. Use a hard-wired (Ethernet) connection and have a back-up plan. If your internet at home is weak, try using a cellphone hotspot.
4. Update your internet browser. Be sure to stay up-to-date with internet browsers that support the applications you need for online learning.
5. Back-up your files. Be sure to save your files each time you work online.
6. Limit video use. Excessive video use may slow a connection. Use only when necessary to avoid technical difficulties.

ACE TIME MANAGEMENT:
The most challenging aspect of taking online courses is time management and paying attention. It's easy to get distracted, access other sites and fall off track.

1. Understand course time requirements & expectations. Ask the professor how many hours you are expected to devote to the course per week.
2. Schedule specific times for your coursework. Inform people of your schedule and do your very best to stick to it.
3. Be sure to note assignment due dates. Print out a course calendar at the beginning of the term with all important due dates clearly noted.
4. Avoid procrastination. Constantly monitor your progress. Each time you sit down to work on your course, make a list of what you plan to get done and a target time to finish the work.

GET FAMILIAR WITH ONLINE LEARNING TERMS:
Every industry has its own terminology, but luckily it's not rocket science to understand that of eLearning. Don't let acronyms and IT jargon hinder your understanding of the tech services, tools and resources that can make your work and life more streamlined.

STAY SAFE ONLINE:
The UIC Information Security and Privacy Office has received many reports of email and telephone scams targeting UIC's community of local and international faculty, students, and staff.

Learn more about avoiding phishing scams directed at the UIC community.

Visit our Technology Basics for Academic Continuity page for more information on acquiring internet access, software resources, online collaboration tools!
